text: Hi — I just invited 8 people to my project and I'm not sure they know what to do next. Is there anything Asana sends them to help them get started? I don't want to have to walk each person through it manually.
reply: Hi! When you invite someone to a project, they receive an email with a link to join. Once they accept, they'll land on the project directly. Asana doesn't send a guided tour to new members by default, but you can share this getting-started guide with your team: [link]. You can also send a message from within Asana by using the Message button in the project header. Let us know if you have other questions!

text: My recurring task keeps appearing on the wrong day. I set it to recur weekly but it's showing up on Thursday instead of Monday. I've re-set it twice and it keeps doing this.
reply: Thanks for reaching out! This is a known behavior — recurring tasks in Asana recur from the date they were completed, not from a fixed day of the week. So if the task was completed on a Thursday, the next instance appears the following Thursday. To get a Monday recurrence, you'd need to complete the task on a Monday (or adjust the due date on the newly created instance before completing it). We understand this is confusing and have passed this feedback to our product team. Apologies for the friction!

text: How do I see all tasks that are overdue across all my projects at once? I've looked everywhere and can only see overdue tasks one project at a time.
reply: Great question — you can do this with Advanced Search! Click the magnifying glass in the top bar, then click 'Advanced Search.' Set the filters: Due date = on or before today, Completion = Incomplete, and leave Assignee as 'Any.' This will return all overdue tasks across your workspace. You can also save this search for quick access in the future by clicking 'Save search' after running it.

text: Can you add a way for project admins to set default notification preferences for team members? Every time I add someone to a project they end up with all notifications on and then they mute everything because it's too much. I want to be able to set something reasonable as the default so new members don't immediately get overwhelmed.
reply: Thank you for this suggestion! Default notification settings per project is not currently available, but it's a request we hear often. I've logged this as a feature request with your details attached. In the meantime, you can share our guide on managing notification preferences with new teammates so they can configure them right after joining. We appreciate you taking the time to write in!

text: What's the difference between 'Private' and 'Comment-only' access when I invite someone to a project? The descriptions are confusing.
reply: Happy to clarify! 'Private' refers to the visibility of the project itself — a private project is only visible to members who have been explicitly invited; it won't show up for anyone else in your organization. 'Comment-only' is a permission level for individual members — it means the person can view tasks and leave comments, but cannot create, edit, or delete tasks. You can use both together: a private project where some members have comment-only access.

text: I duplicated a project to use it as a template for a new project but the copy brought over all the completed tasks. I only wanted the incomplete tasks and the structure. Is there a setting I missed?
reply: When you duplicate a project in Asana, there is an option to choose what gets copied — tasks, members, and task assignments. However, filtering by completion status (i.e., only copying incomplete tasks) is not currently an option in the duplication flow. The workaround is to duplicate the project, then manually delete the completed tasks in the copy, or to convert your project into a proper Template (available on Premium and above) which gives you more control over what's included. Sorry for the inconvenience!

text: I've submitted two feature requests in the past year and both times I get the same copy-paste response saying you've 'logged my feedback.' Is there any way to actually track the status of feature requests? I have no idea if anything is being considered.
reply: I completely understand the frustration, and I want to be transparent with you: we don't currently have a public roadmap where individual feature requests are tracked and visible to users. Your feedback does go to our product team and they review high-volume requests when prioritizing. I know that's not the visibility you're looking for. If you're interested in participating in our product research panel (where we actually interview customers about upcoming features), I can add you to the list. Would that help?

text: Is there a way to assign a task to multiple people? I have a task that two people are responsible for and I can't figure out how to give it to both of them.
reply: Asana tasks can only have one assignee at a time — this is intentional, as the single-owner model is meant to establish clear accountability. For tasks where multiple people are involved, the recommended approach is to: (1) assign the task to the primary owner, and (2) add the other person as a collaborator (click 'Add followers' in the task detail). Collaborators get notified of activity on the task but aren't listed as assignees. Alternatively, you can duplicate the task for each person. We know this isn't always ideal and appreciate your patience!

text: Hi, I'm the IT admin at our company. We just rolled out Asana to 200 users. Is there a way to pre-configure Asana settings for all users (like default notification preferences, default project view, etc.) before they log in for the first time? We're trying to reduce the support burden during rollout.
reply: Welcome to Asana! For enterprise deployments, some admin-level controls are available through the Admin Console, including SAML SSO, domain claiming, and member management. However, pre-configuring individual user preferences (like notification settings or default views) at the admin level is not currently supported. Each user's preferences are personal and must be set by the user. Our Enterprise Success team can work with you on a rollout playbook to help guide users through setup — I'd recommend reaching out to your account executive about that resource.

text: When I'm in a Board view, tasks I complete disappear from the board immediately. This is confusing because I can't see what I just marked done. It feels like the task got deleted. Can I make completed tasks stay visible on the board?
reply: This is expected behavior — when a task is marked complete in Board view, it moves out of the active column. It hasn't been deleted; you can find it by enabling 'Show completed tasks' in the View settings (click 'View' at the top right of the project, then toggle 'Completed tasks'). We understand this can be disorienting at first and have passed your feedback to the team about making this behavior clearer during initial setup.

text: Please add the ability to see task count and completion percentage directly on the project card in the sidebar. When I have 8 projects in my sidebar I have no quick way to assess which ones are behind. I have to click into each one.
reply: Thank you — this is a great idea and you're not the first to request it. A progress indicator on the sidebar or project list view is something our team has explored. I've added your vote to this request. In the meantime, the Portfolios feature shows completion percentage across projects in a single view — if you're not already using it, it might partially solve this problem. Happy to send you instructions for setting it up!

text: Your Asana Academy is too long. I signed up for a tool, not a certification program. I just need to know how to do basic things quickly. Can you make a 10-minute version for people who just want to get something done?
reply: Totally fair feedback, and you're not alone — we hear this often. In addition to Asana Academy, we do have shorter resources: the Quick Start guides (under Help > Get started) are designed for exactly this use case. There are also short video walkthroughs on our YouTube channel for specific tasks like setting up your first project or using My Tasks. I'll also pass your feedback to the team — a 'fast track' onboarding path is something we're actively thinking about.

text: I set up a rule that marks tasks as complete when they're moved to the 'Done' section. But now when I manually complete a task in a different section, it doesn't move to Done. I want it to work both ways — move to Done when complete AND complete when moved to Done.
reply: You can set up two rules to achieve this! Rule 1: 'When a task is moved to the Done section → Mark the task complete.' Rule 2: 'When a task is marked complete → Move to Done section.' These rules work together and handle both trigger directions. One thing to watch: if both rules fire at once (e.g., you complete a task that's already in Done), Asana is smart enough not to create a loop, but you may want to test it with a dummy task first. Let me know if you run into any issues!

text: I was working in Asana on my phone and marked a task complete. Then I opened the desktop app and the task was still showing as incomplete. I had to refresh the page. Is this a sync issue?
reply: Thanks for reporting this! In most cases, Asana syncs in real-time between devices, but occasionally there can be a brief delay, especially on mobile if the app goes into the background before the action fully syncs. A manual refresh (pull-to-refresh on mobile, or F5 on desktop) should always show the current state. If you're seeing consistent delays of more than a minute, it may be worth checking your mobile app version and internet connection. Please let us know if it keeps happening — we'd want to log it as a potential bug.

text: How do I create a project template? I've set up a project structure that works well and I want to reuse it without duplicating the whole project every time.
reply: To create a Template, open your project, click the three-dot menu in the project header, and select 'Save as template.' You can then access your saved templates when creating a new project — they appear under 'My templates' in the template selection screen. Note: the ability to save custom templates requires a Premium plan or above. If you're on the free plan, project duplication is the best alternative for now. Happy to walk you through either option!

text: I find it really hard to explain to new team members what the difference between a Task, Subtask, Sub-subtask, and Section is. The hierarchy makes sense to me now but it confused everyone I've added to the team. Is there a visual explainer I can share with them?
reply: This is a common sticking point and you're right that the hierarchy can be confusing at first! We have a visual overview of Asana's structure here: [help link]. The short version: Sections are headers that organize tasks within a project; Tasks are the core unit of work; Subtasks break a task into smaller pieces; Sub-subtasks go one level deeper (though we generally recommend avoiding this level of nesting as tasks can become hard to find). I'll also pass your feedback — a clearer in-product explanation for new members is something we're aware of.

text: I manage multiple clients as a freelancer and I use separate Asana workspaces for each one. The biggest problem is that I can't see my tasks across all workspaces in one place. I have to switch workspaces to check each one. This is a huge daily pain point. Is cross-workspace task view something you're planning?
reply: Thank you for sharing this — it's a real pain point for freelancers and consultants who manage multiple workspaces. Currently, My Tasks and cross-project views are scoped to a single workspace. I don't have timeline information I can share on whether cross-workspace views are planned, but this is one of our most-requested features from independent practitioners and I've added your name to the request. In the meantime, some users consolidate client work into a single workspace using separate teams and privacy settings, though I know that's not ideal for everyone.

text: I accidentally deleted a task. Can I undo this? I don't see an undo button anywhere.
reply: Yes, you can restore it! Deleted tasks go to your project's Trash, accessible from the three-dot menu in the project header — click 'View trash.' From there you can restore any recently deleted task. You can also use Ctrl+Z (or Cmd+Z on Mac) immediately after deletion to undo it, as long as you haven't navigated away. If the task has been in trash for more than 30 days it will be permanently deleted, but you likely have plenty of time. Let me know if you can't find it!

text: We're a team of 5 and we hit the 15-project limit on the free plan. We can't afford the Business tier ($$$) but the free plan is too restricted. Is there a small team or startup pricing option? The pricing gap between free and paid feels enormous.
reply: Thank you for writing in, and I hear you on the pricing gap. We do offer a discounted rate for eligible nonprofits, and there's an annual billing option that reduces the monthly cost. For very small teams, the Premium plan (lower than Business) may be worth reviewing — it includes unlimited projects without some of the more advanced features. I'd encourage you to speak with our sales team as they sometimes have options for early-stage startups. I'm sorry I can't offer a specific discount here, but I want to make sure you have all the options.

text: What does the little lightning bolt icon mean on some tasks? I've seen it appear and disappear and I have no idea what triggers it.
reply: The lightning bolt icon indicates that the task was created or modified by an automation rule (also called a 'trigger action'). It's a signal that something automated happened to that task rather than a manual user action. If you're seeing it unexpectedly, it means a rule in your project fired on that task — you can review your project's rules under 'Customize' to see which rules are active. If you didn't set up any rules intentionally, it's possible a template or someone else with edit access created one. Let me know if you'd like help finding it!

text: Hi — we're planning to cancel our subscription and I need to export all our project data first. We have about 12 active projects and 3 years of completed work. Can you walk me through how to export everything? I want task names, descriptions, comments, assignees, and due dates if possible. || FOLLOW-UP (2 days later): Thanks for the instructions — I followed the steps but the export only seems to include tasks from one project at a time. I have 12 projects. Is there a way to export everything in one go? Also the CSV doesn't include task comments, just the other fields. Is that normal?
reply: REPLY 1: Hi! To export a project, open it, click the three-dot menu in the project header, and select 'Export / Print.' You can choose CSV or JSON. The CSV includes task names, assignees, due dates, sections, and descriptions. For comments, the JSON format is more complete. Repeat this for each project. If you're on Business or above, you may also have access to the Admin Console for broader data exports. Let me know how it goes! || REPLY 2: You're correct — project exports are per-project, and there's no bulk export for all projects simultaneously. For 12 projects you would need to export each one individually. Regarding comments: CSV exports do not include comments; the JSON export includes the full task object with comments attached. If you need a single combined file, you'd need to merge the JSON exports manually or use the Asana API (documentation here: [link]). I understand this isn't the smoothest process for a large export — I've flagged this as feedback to our product team. I'm sorry the offboarding experience wasn't smoother.

text: I'm trying to understand our invoice. We have 14 seats but the invoice shows 17. We didn't add anyone this month — where are the extra 3 seats coming from? || FOLLOW-UP (1 day later): Thanks for the explanation about guest vs member seats. Quick follow-up: two of the 'members' you identified are contractors who only need occasional access. Can I downgrade them to guests without losing their task history or comments? I want to make sure we don't accidentally lose any of their work before I change anything.
reply: REPLY 1: Happy to help clarify! In Asana, a 'seat' refers to a full member of your workspace — anyone who has been added as a member (not a guest). Guests have limited access and don't count toward your seat total. Looking at your account, it appears 3 users were upgraded from guest to member status in the past billing cycle — this can happen if a guest accepts certain types of project invitations that trigger a membership upgrade. I'll list those 3 accounts in a follow-up message so you can confirm. || REPLY 2: Good news — you can convert a full member back to a guest without losing their task history, comments, or assignments. Their completed and open tasks remain intact; they just lose the ability to create projects or access workspace-level features. To do this: go to Admin Console > Members, find the user, and change their role to Guest. I'd still recommend reviewing their open task assignments before downgrading, just to make sure nothing falls through the cracks. Let me know if you need help walking through it!

text: We have a new employee starting Monday. I want to make sure she's set up in Asana and knows what to do on her first day. What's the best way to onboard a new team member? Should I add her before she starts, assign her tasks in advance, and is there any kind of welcome flow she'll go through when she first logs in? || FOLLOW-UP (4 days later): Quick update — she joined on Monday and said the experience was confusing. She accepted the invite, got into Asana, saw a project, and had no idea what she was supposed to do first. Is there something I should have done differently to set her up better? She also said she got a lot of emails immediately and nearly unsubscribed from all notifications.
reply: REPLY 1: Great that you're thinking about this in advance! Here's what I'd recommend: (1) Add her to Asana before Monday so she can accept the invite and get oriented. (2) Assign her a few concrete tasks with due dates in the first week — having her name on specific tasks makes My Tasks useful immediately. (3) Consider writing a brief comment on her first task explaining what you want her to do and how you're using Asana as a team. (4) Send her this quick-start guide [link] along with the invite. There's no formal welcome flow for new members beyond the invite email, so the context you set up manually matters a lot. || REPLY 2: Thanks for the follow-up — what you're describing is unfortunately a common experience. The new member invite flow doesn't include an orientation or a 'here's what to do first' prompt. A few things that help: (1) When you add someone to a project, leave a welcome comment on their first task. (2) Ask her to go to My Tasks first (not Home or a project) — it's the cleanest view for someone just starting out. (3) For notifications: walk her through Settings > Notifications and turn off email notifications for everything except @mentions and task assignments. That usually cuts the noise dramatically. I'd also pass along that a guided new-member experience is something we hear often as a gap — your feedback has been logged.
